explanation.title: What is Python and Why Should You Learn It?
explanation.content:
# What is Python?

Imagine you want to give instructions to a very precise robot. The robot does exactly what you say, but it only understands one specific language. If you speak in that language correctly, the robot obeys every instruction perfectly. **Python** is one of those languages — but instead of a robot, you are giving instructions to a computer.

More formally, Python is a **programming language** — a set of words and rules that lets humans write instructions a computer can execute. It was created by Guido van Rossum and first released in 1991. The name comes from the British comedy show *Monty Python's Flying Circus*, not from the snake.

## Why Python Is Special

There are hundreds of programming languages in the world. Python stands out for several reasons that make it the most popular choice for beginners and professionals alike.

- **Readable syntax** — Python code looks almost like plain English sentences, which means you spend less time decoding the language and more time solving real problems
- **Versatile** — the same language is used for web development, artificial intelligence, data analysis, automation, and game development
- **Huge ecosystem** — Python has thousands of free libraries (pre-written code packages) that let you build powerful things quickly
- **Strong job market** — Python developers are among the most in-demand professionals in India and worldwide

## Where Python Is Used Today

| Industry | Real-World Example |
|---|---|
| Artificial Intelligence | ChatGPT and image recognition systems are built with Python |
| Web Development | Instagram and Pinterest run on Python backends |
| Data Science | Banks and research institutions analyse data with Python |
| Automation | Repetitive tasks like filling forms or renaming files are automated with Python scripts |
| Education | Python is the first language taught at MIT, IITs, and most engineering colleges |

## Python Compared to Other Languages

You may have heard of other programming languages. Here is how Python compares:

| Feature | Python | Java | C++ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Beginner-friendly | Very easy | Moderate | Difficult |
| Lines to print "Hello" | 1 line | 5 lines | 4 lines |
| Main use | Everything | Enterprise, Android | System software, games |
| Typing style | Dynamic | Static | Static |

The key insight is that Python lets you express ideas in fewer lines of code than almost any other language, which means you build things faster and make fewer mistakes.

In the code example below, you will see your first Python program — a simple set of `print()` statements that demonstrate how natural Python code reads compared to other languages.

codeExample.title: Python Says Hello to the World
codeExample.code:
# Welcome to Python! Lines starting with # are comments.
# Python ignores comments — they are notes for you, the programmer.

# The print() function displays text on the screen.
print("Hello, World!")

# You can print any text you like inside the quotes.
print("My name is CodeGuru AI.")
print("I am written in Python.")

# Python can also do arithmetic directly inside print().
print("The year Python was created:", 1991)
print("Years of Python so far:", 2025 - 1991)

# You can print multiple items by separating them with commas.
print("Python", "is", "awesome!")
codeExample.explanation:
- `# comment text` — the `#` symbol starts a comment; Python ignores everything after it on that line
- `print()` — the built-in function that sends output to the screen; everything inside the parentheses gets displayed
- `"text in quotes"` — text surrounded by double quotes is called a **string**; strings are a fundamental data type
- `print("label:", 1991)` — commas inside `print()` separate items; Python adds a single space between each automatically
- `2025 - 1991` — Python evaluates arithmetic expressions before printing; the output shows the result `34`, not the expression

exercise.title: Write Your Personal Introduction Program
exercise.instructions:
Write a Python program that introduces you. It must print exactly four lines: your name on the first line, your city on the second line, your age as a number on the third line, and your favorite subject on the fourth line. Each line must be printed using its own print() statement. Example expected output:
Rahul Sharma
Delhi
20
Computer Science
exercise.starterCode:
# Personal Introduction Program
# Replace each placeholder with your actual information

# Line 1: Print your full name
print("Your Name Here")

# Line 2: Print your city
print("Your City Here")

# Line 3: Print your age as a number (no quotes needed)
# Your code here

# Line 4: Print your favorite subject
# Your code here
exercise.solutionCode:
# Personal Introduction Program

print("Rahul Sharma")
print("Delhi")
print(20)
print("Computer Science")
hints: 💡 Each piece of information needs its own print() statement on a separate line.
💡 Text values like names and cities go inside quotes, but a number like your age does not need quotes.
💡 Copy the pattern of the first two print() statements and write two more below them for age and subject.

questions.question: What is Python most accurately described as?
options: A type of snake used as a programming mascot
A programming language that gives instructions to a computer
A software application you download from the internet
A hardware component inside a computer
questions.explanation: Python is a programming language — a set of words and rules that allows humans to write instructions that a computer can understand and execute. It is not hardware, an application, or named after the snake (it is named after Monty Python's Flying Circus).

questions.question: What does the # symbol do at the start of a Python line?
options: It multiplies the numbers that follow it
It causes a syntax error and stops the program
It starts a comment that Python completely ignores
It prints the rest of the line to the screen
questions.explanation: The # symbol starts a comment. Python ignores everything on that line after the # character. Comments are notes written by the programmer for other humans reading the code — they have no effect on how the program runs.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly prints the word Hello to the screen in Python?
options: print Hello
PRINT("Hello")
print("Hello")
display("Hello")
questions.explanation: print("Hello") is the correct syntax. The function name must be lowercase — Python is case-sensitive, so PRINT or Print will cause a NameError. The text to display must be inside parentheses and surrounded by quotes. There is no display() function in Python's standard library.

explanation.title: How to Install Python and Run Your First Program
explanation.content:
# Installing Python

Before you can run Python programs on your own computer, you need to install two things: **Python itself** (the engine that reads and executes your code) and a place to write your code (a text editor or IDE). Think of it like this — Python is the engine of a car, and your code editor is the steering wheel and dashboard you use to control it.

## Step 1 — Download Python

Python is completely free. To download it:

1. Open your web browser and go to `python.org/downloads`
2. The website automatically detects your operating system and recommends the correct version
3. Click the yellow **Download Python 3.x.x** button (always choose Python 3, never Python 2)
4. Run the installer file that downloads

## Step 2 — Install Python (Windows)

When the Windows installer opens, you will see a checkbox at the bottom that says **"Add Python to PATH"**. This is the most important step — check that box before clicking Install Now. Without it, your computer will not be able to find Python when you type commands.

For macOS and Linux users, Python 3 may already be installed. Open a terminal and type `python3 --version` to check.

## Step 3 — Verify the Installation

After installation, verify everything worked correctly:

- On **Windows**: press `Win + R`, type `cmd`, press Enter, then type `python --version`
- On **macOS/Linux**: open Terminal and type `python3 --version`

If Python installed correctly, you will see output like `Python 3.12.0`. If you see an error, revisit Step 2 and make sure you checked the PATH checkbox.

## Step 4 — Choose a Code Editor

You can write Python in any text editor, but a dedicated code editor makes the experience much better. The best free options are:

| Editor | Best for | Download |
|---|---|---|
| **VS Code** | All skill levels, most popular | `code.visualstudio.com` |
| **IDLE** | Absolute beginners, comes with Python | Already installed |
| **PyCharm Community** | Intermediate learners | `jetbrains.com/pycharm` |

For this course, you can use the built-in code editor right here — no installation needed. But installing Python locally is a valuable skill you will use throughout your programming career.

## Running a Python File

Once Python and an editor are installed, you run a Python file from the terminal:

```
python hello.py
```

This command tells Python to find the file named `hello.py` and execute every line inside it from top to bottom.

In the code example below, you will see what a complete Python source file looks like and how Python executes the instructions inside it in order.

codeExample.title: Python Installation Verifier Program
codeExample.code:
# This program confirms Python is working correctly.
# It also demonstrates that Python executes line by line, top to bottom.

import sys   # sys is a standard library module — always available

# Print the Python version currently running
print("Python version:", sys.version)

# Print the platform (operating system)
print("Platform:", sys.platform)

# Demonstrate that Python runs top to bottom
print("Step 1: Python found the file")
print("Step 2: Python read the import statement")
print("Step 3: Python executed each print() in order")
print("Step 4: Program complete — Python stops here")

# Python's built-in functions are always available without importing
print("Built-in function example:", abs(-42))   # abs() gives absolute value
print("Another built-in:", len("Hello"))         # len() counts characters
codeExample.explanation:
- `import sys` — imports the `sys` module from Python's standard library; modules are collections of pre-written functions
- `sys.version` — a property of `sys` containing the exact Python version string; useful for verifying which Python is running
- `sys.platform` — returns a string identifying the OS: `'win32'` for Windows, `'darwin'` for macOS, `'linux'` for Linux
- The five `print()` calls show that Python executes instructions top to bottom, one line at a time, with no skipping
- `abs(-42)` and `len("Hello")` are **built-in functions** — always available without any import statement

exercise.title: Explore Your Python Environment
exercise.instructions:
Write a program that uses the sys module to display information about your Python installation. The program must: (1) import the sys module, (2) print the Python version using sys.version, (3) print the platform using sys.platform, (4) print the path to the Python executable using sys.executable. Each item must be printed on its own labeled line. Expected output format:
Version: [version info]
Platform: [platform name]
Executable: [path to python]
exercise.starterCode:
# Explore Your Python Environment
# Import the sys module to access system information
import sys

# Print the Python version with the label "Version:"
print("Version:", sys.version)

# Print the platform with the label "Platform:"
# Your code here

# Print the Python executable path with the label "Executable:"
# Your code here
exercise.solutionCode:
# Explore Your Python Environment
import sys

print("Version:",    sys.version)
print("Platform:",   sys.platform)
print("Executable:", sys.executable)
hints: 💡 The sys module must be imported before you can access sys.platform or sys.executable.
💡 All three attributes follow the same pattern: sys.version, sys.platform, sys.executable — each is accessed with a dot after sys.
💡 Copy the pattern of the first print statement exactly — replace sys.version with sys.platform and then sys.executable for the remaining two lines.

questions.question: When installing Python on Windows, which option must you enable to use Python from the command line?
options: Install for all users
Add Python to PATH
Install pip separately
Enable Python launcher
questions.explanation: The 'Add Python to PATH' checkbox is the most critical option during Windows installation. PATH is a system variable that lists directories where Windows looks for executable programs. Without adding Python to PATH, your computer cannot find the python command and you will get a 'command not found' error every time you try to run a Python program from the terminal.

questions.question: What command do you type in a terminal to confirm that Python installed correctly?
options: python --check
python --version
check python
python --install
questions.explanation: python --version (or python3 --version on macOS and Linux) asks Python to report its version number. If Python is installed correctly and on PATH, you will see output like 'Python 3.12.0'. If you see an error such as 'python is not recognized', Python was not added to PATH during installation.

questions.question: What does the import sys statement at the top of a Python file do?
options: Downloads the sys package from the internet
Creates a new module called sys
Makes the sys module's functions and variables available in your program
Saves a backup copy of your file
questions.explanation: The import statement loads a module and makes its contents available in your program. The sys module is part of Python's standard library — it comes with every Python installation and never needs to be downloaded. After writing import sys, you can access sys.version, sys.platform, sys.executable, and many other system properties using dot notation.

explanation.title: Writing, Understanding, and Running Your First Real Python Program
explanation.content:
# Your First Python Program

Every programmer remembers writing their first real program. It is a milestone — the moment a computer does something because *you* told it to. In this lesson, you will write a program that collects information and presents it in a formatted way. Before writing any code, let us understand the building blocks you will use.

## The print() Function

You have already seen `print()`. It is Python's most fundamental output tool — it takes whatever you give it and displays it on the screen.

```python
print("Welcome to CodeGuru AI")   # displays text
print(42)                          # displays a number
print(3.14)                        # displays a decimal
```

You can print multiple values on one line by separating them with commas:

```python
print("Score:", 95, "out of", 100)
```
Output: `Score: 95 out of 100`

Python automatically adds a single space between each comma-separated item.

## Variables — Storing Information

A **variable** is a named container that stores a value so you can use it later. Creating a variable in Python takes one line:

```python
student_name = "Rahul Sharma"
course       = "Python Basics"
score        = 87
```

The `=` sign here is the **assignment operator** — it puts the value on the right side into the container on the left side. It does not mean 'equals' in the mathematical sense.

## Strings — Working With Text

Text values in Python are called **strings**. A string is any sequence of characters surrounded by quotes. You can use single quotes `'text'` or double quotes `"text"` — both work identically.

One of the most useful string features is the **f-string** (formatted string literal), which lets you embed variable values directly inside text:

```python
name  = "Priya"
marks = 92
print(f"Student {name} scored {marks} marks.")
```
Output: `Student Priya scored 92 marks.`

The `f` before the opening quote activates the f-string. Variables inside `{}` braces are replaced with their values automatically.

## Comments — Notes for Humans

A **comment** starts with `#` and extends to the end of the line. Python ignores comments completely. They are notes you write to explain your code to yourself and to anyone else who reads it later.

```python
# This calculates the final price after tax
price    = 1000
tax_rate = 0.18                     # 18% GST
final    = price + (price * tax_rate)
```

Writing clear comments is a professional habit. Code without comments is like a recipe without instructions — the ingredients are there but the method is unclear.

## Putting It Together

In the code example below, you will see a complete program that creates a student report card using variables, f-strings, and comments. It demonstrates how these three elements work together to produce readable, meaningful output.

codeExample.title: Student Report Card Generator
codeExample.code:
# ── Student Report Card Generator ──────────────────────────
# This program creates a formatted report card for a student.

# Step 1: Store student information in variables
student_name   = "Ananya Krishnan"   # string — student's full name
roll_number    = 42                   # integer — class roll number
city           = "Bangalore"          # string — home city

# Step 2: Store subject marks in variables
maths_marks    = 91    # out of 100
science_marks  = 88    # out of 100
english_marks  = 84    # out of 100

# Step 3: Calculate total and average
total_marks    = maths_marks + science_marks + english_marks
average_marks  = total_marks / 3      # division always gives a decimal in Python 3

# Step 4: Print the report card using f-strings
print("╔══════════════════════════════╗")
print("║       STUDENT REPORT CARD   ║")
print("╠══════════════════════════════╣")
print(f"  Name      : {student_name}")
print(f"  Roll No.  : {roll_number}")
print(f"  City      : {city}")
print("──────────────────────────────")
print(f"  Maths     : {maths_marks}/100")
print(f"  Science   : {science_marks}/100")
print(f"  English   : {english_marks}/100")
print("──────────────────────────────")
print(f"  Total     : {total_marks}/300")
print(f"  Average   : {average_marks:.1f}%")  # :.1f formats to 1 decimal place
print("╚══════════════════════════════╝")
codeExample.explanation:
- `student_name = "Ananya Krishnan"` — creates a string variable; stored and reused wherever the name appears later
- `total_marks = maths_marks + science_marks + english_marks` — Python evaluates the arithmetic and stores the result
- `average_marks = total_marks / 3` — in Python 3 the `/` operator always returns a decimal, even when dividing whole numbers
- `f"  Name : {student_name}"` — the `f` prefix enables variable substitution; anything inside `{}` becomes the variable's value
- `{average_marks:.1f}` — the `:.1f` format specifier rounds to exactly one decimal place before displaying

exercise.title: Build Your Own Report Card
exercise.instructions:
Create a report card program for a student of your choice. Define variables for: a student name (string), a roll number (integer), and marks for three subjects (integers, each out of 100). Calculate the total marks and the average. Print a report showing all information.

Required output must include:
- Student name and roll number
- All three subject marks
- Total and average rounded to one decimal place

Example: a student with marks 88, 75, 92 has a total of 255 and average of 85.0.
exercise.starterCode:
# My Report Card Program

# Step 1: Define student information
student_name = "Your Name Here"    # replace with any name
roll_number  = 1                    # replace with any number

# Step 2: Define three subject marks (integers from 0 to 100)
# Choose any three subjects you like
subject1_marks = 0    # replace with a mark
subject2_marks = 0    # replace with a mark
subject3_marks = 0    # replace with a mark

# Step 3: Calculate total and average
total   = subject1_marks + subject2_marks + subject3_marks
average = total / 3

# Step 4: Print the report card
# Use f-strings to include variable values in your output
print(f"Student: {student_name}")
print(f"Roll No: {roll_number}")
# Print the three subject marks here — add three more print statements

# Print the total and average here
print(f"Total: {total}/300")
# Your code here for average
exercise.solutionCode:
# My Report Card Program

student_name   = "Vikram Mehta"
roll_number    = 7
subject1_marks = 88
subject2_marks = 75
subject3_marks = 92

total   = subject1_marks + subject2_marks + subject3_marks
average = total / 3

print(f"Student : {student_name}")
print(f"Roll No : {roll_number}")
print(f"Maths   : {subject1_marks}/100")
print(f"Science : {subject2_marks}/100")
print(f"English : {subject3_marks}/100")
print(f"Total   : {total}/300")
print(f"Average : {average:.1f}%")
hints: 💡 Replace the 0 placeholder values with real numbers between 0 and 100 for each subject mark.
💡 Use an f-string to print each subject mark — write f"Subject: {subject1_marks}/100" and repeat the same pattern for the other two subjects.
💡 To print the average rounded to one decimal place, use the format specifier :.1f inside the f-string braces: f"Average: {average:.1f}%"

questions.question: What does the = operator do in the statement score = 87?
options: Tests whether score is equal to 87
Stores the value 87 in a variable named score
Prints the number 87 to the screen
Declares score as a constant that cannot change
questions.explanation: In Python, = is the assignment operator — it stores the value on its right side into the variable named on its left side. It does not compare values. To compare values in Python, you use == (two equals signs). After writing score = 87, the variable score holds the integer 87 and you can use it anywhere in your program.

questions.question: What is an f-string in Python and how do you create one?
options: A string that stores floating-point numbers — created by writing float("text")
A formatted string that embeds variable values — created by placing f before the opening quote
A string stored in a file — created by writing file("text")
A function string that executes code — created by placing func before the opening quote
questions.explanation: An f-string (formatted string literal) embeds variable values directly inside text. You create one by placing the letter f before the opening quote: f"text {variable} more text". Anything inside curly braces is replaced with the variable's value when displayed. F-strings were introduced in Python 3.6 and are the recommended way to format output strings.

questions.question: What will Python output when it executes total = 91 + 88 + 84 followed by print(total)?
options: total
An error because total was not defined before use
questions.explanation: Python evaluates the arithmetic expression 91 + 88 + 84 first, which equals 263, and stores that result in the variable total. When print(total) is executed, Python looks up the value stored in total and displays 263. Python never prints the variable name itself or the original expression — it always prints the resolved value.
